methodology: Cross-referenced USDA FoodData Central, FatSecret, CalorieKing, and published nutrition data from major steakhouses (LongHorn, Outback, Ruth's Chris, Capital Grille, Texas Roadhouse). Steak nutrition based on USDA cooked values for Choice grade beef, broiled/grilled, trimmed to 1/8 inch fat (typical restaurant prep). All steaks are 8oz COOKED weight unless noted. Restaurant sides cross-referenced against LongHorn and Outback published data.
coaching_insight: At a steakhouse, the steak is NOT the problem — the sides are. A filet mignon (440 cal, 50g protein) is excellent. But add a loaded baked potato (470 cal), creamed spinach (200 cal), bread basket (400 cal), and a Caesar salad (450 cal) and you're at 1960 cal before dessert. EAMON'S RULE: 'loin or round = lean.' Filet mignon = 440 cal per 8oz. Ribeye = 780 cal per 8oz. Same protein, 340 cal difference from fat marbling.

items.notes: THE LEANEST STEAKHOUSE CUT. Tenderloin — extremely tender, low marbling. FatSecret: 48 cal/oz cooked = 384 for 8oz (lean end). USDA Choice tenderloin broiled: ~55 cal/oz with typical fat = 440. Restaurant filets are often wrapped in bacon (add 70 cal) or topped with butter (add 100 cal). Ask for neither.
items.lean_version: Already the lean king. No bacon wrap, no butter on top. Ask for it grilled with just salt and pepper. Best protein-to-calorie ratio of any steak.

items.notes: Best VALUE lean cut — cheaper than filet, similar macros. Top sirloin is leaner than bottom sirloin. USDA top sirloin broiled: ~60 cal/oz. Good protein-to-calorie ratio. Often the 'budget' steak on steakhouse menus but nutritionally one of the best choices.
items.lean_version: Request top sirloin specifically. No compound butter. Already lean — 'loin' in the name = lean cut.

items.notes: Strip loin — moderate marbling, good beefy flavor. More fat than filet or sirloin but less than ribeye. FatSecret/USDA: varies from 260-294 cal per 4oz depending on trim. A 'middle of the road' choice. The fat cap on one side can be trimmed to save 50+ cal.
items.lean_version: Trim the visible fat cap on the edge. No butter. Still has 'loin' in the name (strip LOIN) so it's a reasonable choice — 140 cal more than filet but 200 cal less than ribeye.

items.notes: THE FATTIEST COMMON CUT. Heavy marbling throughout — the fat is IN the meat, you can't trim it. FatSecret: ~71 cal/oz for cooked ribeye with fat. Eamon's rule: same 48g protein as filet, but 340 MORE CALORIES purely from fat marbling. If someone says 'I eat healthy, I had a steak' — ask which cut. Ribeye ≠ filet.
items.lean_version: Switch to filet mignon (saves 340 cal) or NY strip (saves 200 cal). If you must have ribeye for the flavor, get a 6oz instead of 8oz (saves 195 cal). The intramuscular fat can't be trimmed away.

items.notes: Slow-roasted rib roast — similar fat content to ribeye (same primal cut). Texas Roadhouse 12oz = 950 cal, so 8oz ~635-720 cal. Often served with au jus (30 cal) and horseradish cream (60 cal per tbsp). The end cut is leaner than the center cut.
items.lean_version: Ask for the END CUT (leaner, less marbling). Skip the horseradish cream. Au jus is fine (mostly beef broth). Or just order filet instead — saves 280 cal.

items.notes: Two steaks in one: NY strip on one side, tenderloin on the other, separated by a T-shaped bone. FatSecret: 264 cal per 3oz (trimmed to 1/4 inch fat) = ~700 for 8oz with fat, ~620 trimmed. Usually served as 16-20oz cuts — an 8oz portion is half the steak. Porterhouse has a larger tenderloin portion than T-bone.
items.lean_version: Eat the tenderloin side first (leaner). Share the strip side or take it home. A full porterhouse is 1200-1500 cal — splitting it is smart.

items.notes: Atlantic salmon, grilled. Higher fat than white fish but it's omega-3 rich (heart-healthy fats). USDA: 58 cal/oz for cooked Atlantic salmon. Steakhouse salmon is often glazed with butter or honey (add 80-150 cal). One of the best non-steak options.
items.lean_version: Ask for grilled with lemon, no butter or glaze. Saves 80-150 cal. The fat in salmon is the good kind — don't avoid it, just avoid the ADDED fat.

items.notes: The leanest protein at any steakhouse. USDA chicken breast grilled: ~46 cal/oz. Usually the cheapest entree. Some steakhouses make it well; others treat it as an afterthought. Best protein-to-calorie ratio on the entire menu.
items.lean_version: Already the leanest option. Ask for no butter or oil on the grill. Pair with steamed broccoli and a side salad for a ~500 cal steakhouse meal.

items.notes: Extremely lean protein. The problem is the DRAWN BUTTER it comes with — 2 tbsp = 200 cal of pure fat. Without butter, lobster tail is one of the lightest proteins available. A surf-and-turf (lobster + filet) without butter is still only ~640 cal for both proteins.
items.lean_version: Skip the drawn butter (saves 200+ cal). Use lemon juice and a tiny dip of cocktail sauce instead. The lobster itself is incredibly lean.

items.notes: Grilled jumbo shrimp. Ultra-lean protein, similar to lobster. Great as an add-on to a steak for surf-and-turf. Steakhouse shrimp are often basted in garlic butter (add 60-100 cal). Without butter, shrimp is almost pure protein.
items.lean_version: Ask for grilled with garlic and lemon, no butter. Cocktail shrimp (cold) is even leaner — no cooking fat at all.

items.notes: THE BEST STEAKHOUSE STARTER. Cold boiled shrimp with cocktail sauce. Almost pure protein. Cocktail sauce is mostly tomato/horseradish (~15 cal per tbsp). Compare to bread basket (400 cal) or French onion soup (370 cal). FatSecret: 80-120 cal for shrimp portion + ~40 cal for sauce.
items.lean_version: Already the leanest starter. Order this while everyone else eats the bread basket.

items.notes: Beef broth with caramelized onions, topped with bread and melted Gruyère cheese. The cheese crust alone is 150-200 cal. FatSecret generic: 369 cal/cup. Restaurant versions vary hugely: LongHorn = 170 cal, Outback = 570 cal. The bread soaking in broth + cheese makes this deceptively caloric.
items.lean_version: Order a regular broth-based soup if available. If you must have French onion, eat the broth and onions, leave most of the bread/cheese crust. Or just get the shrimp cocktail instead.

items.notes: Iceberg lettuce wedge with blue cheese dressing, crumbled blue cheese, bacon bits, and sometimes tomatoes. Capital Grille version: 560 cal. The lettuce is basically water — all the calories come from the dressing (77 cal/tbsp), cheese, and bacon. A 'salad' that's 80% fat.
items.lean_version: Ask for dressing on the side and use 1 tbsp (77 cal) instead of the 3-4 tbsp they pour on. Skip the bacon. Or order a simple house salad with vinaigrette (~150 cal).

items.notes: Warm bread rolls with butter. Most people eat 2-4 rolls while waiting for food. Each roll = ~150 cal, each butter pat = ~50 cal. The bread is warm, soft, and arrives when you're hungriest — designed to make you eat more. Texas Roadhouse rolls with cinnamon butter are 230 cal each.
items.lean_version: Ask the server not to bring bread. If it arrives, move it to the other side of the table. If you must have one, eat 1 roll with no butter (~150 cal) and push the basket away. 400 cal saved by just saying 'no bread please.'

items.notes: Romaine lettuce with Caesar dressing (oil, egg, anchovy, Parmesan), croutons, and Parmesan shavings. The dressing is ~80 cal/tbsp and they use 3-4 tbsp. Croutons add 60-80 cal. An entree-size Caesar = 500-700 cal.
items.lean_version: Dressing on the side, use 1 tbsp. Skip croutons. Or order a simple green salad with balsamic vinaigrette (~120 cal).

items.notes: Large baked potato with butter, sour cream, cheese, and bacon bits. The plain potato is only ~280 cal — the toppings add 200+ cal. LongHorn: 470 cal, Outback: 340 cal, Famous Dave's: 640 cal. Huge variation based on topping generosity.
items.lean_version: Get a PLAIN baked potato (~280 cal). Add 1 tbsp butter (100 cal) yourself. Skip cheese, bacon, sour cream. Or get a sweet potato (even better — more fiber, micronutrients).

items.notes: Steakhouse mashed potatoes are made with butter, cream, and sometimes cheese. Homemade is ~200 cal/cup; restaurant versions add 100-200 cal from extra butter and cream. Ruth's Chris mashed potatoes are notorious. Smooth, creamy, and deceptively caloric.
items.lean_version: Swap to a plain baked potato or sweet potato. If you must have mashed, ask for a half portion.

items.notes: Steakhouse mac & cheese uses heavy cream, multiple cheeses, sometimes topped with breadcrumbs and baked. LongHorn: 610 cal per serving. This is a 'side' that has more calories than a filet mignon. The pasta + cheese + cream triple-threat.
items.lean_version: Avoid entirely at a steakhouse. If you want a carb side, get a plain baked potato (280 cal) or sweet potato (180 cal). Mac & cheese as a 'side' turns a 440-cal filet dinner into a 960-cal meal.

items.notes: Spinach in cream sauce (butter, cream, sometimes Parmesan). FatSecret generic: 148 cal/cup. Steakhouse versions are richer — Ruth's Chris is closer to 260 cal. Better than mac & cheese or loaded potato, but the cream adds 80+ cal vs plain spinach (~40 cal/cup).
items.lean_version: Ask for sautéed spinach with garlic (no cream) if available. Saves 100+ cal. Or get steamed broccoli/asparagus instead (~50 cal).

items.notes: Deep-fried potatoes. USDA: ~312 cal per 100g. Steakhouse fries are often thicker steak-cut style. Some places use truffle oil or Parmesan (add 50-100 cal). Restaurant portions are usually larger than 1 cup.
items.lean_version: Switch to a baked potato (280 cal, more filling) or sweet potato (180 cal). If you must have fries, eat half and push the rest away.

items.notes: THE BEST STEAKHOUSE SIDE. Steamed broccoli is 55 cal/cup. Even if the kitchen adds a pat of butter, you're at 105 cal. Compare to mac & cheese (520 cal) or loaded potato (470 cal). This is the highest-volume, lowest-calorie side on the menu. USDA #11091.
items.lean_version: Already the leanest side. Ask for no butter, just lemon. Pair with your steak for the best possible macros.

items.notes: Grilled asparagus spears. Even lighter than broccoli. Steakhouses often drizzle with olive oil and Parmesan (add 60-100 cal). Plain grilled = ~40 cal. Excellent micronutrient profile (folate, vitamin K).
items.lean_version: Ask for grilled with lemon only, no oil or cheese. Already a top-tier side choice.

items.notes: Plain baked sweet potato. Naturally sweet, no toppings needed. More fiber and vitamins than white potato. Steakhouses often serve with brown sugar butter (add 100+ cal) — skip it. The natural sugars are enough.
items.lean_version: Eat plain or with a sprinkle of cinnamon. Skip the brown sugar butter/marshmallow treatment. A plain sweet potato is one of the best carb sources at a steakhouse.

items.notes: Mixed greens with vinaigrette dressing. Much lighter than Caesar or wedge salads. The dressing choice matters: vinaigrette = ~45 cal/tbsp, ranch = ~75 cal/tbsp, blue cheese = ~77 cal/tbsp.
items.lean_version: Already a lean choice. Ask for balsamic vinaigrette on the side. Use 1 tbsp, dip your fork.

items.notes: Pure clarified butter. 100 cal per tablespoon. The ramekin of drawn butter served with lobster and steak is typically 2-3 tbsp (200-300 cal). This single condiment can add more calories than a side of steamed vegetables.
items.lean_version: Skip entirely. Use lemon juice on lobster. On steak, use salt/pepper or a small amount of horseradish. This is the easiest 200 cal to cut at a steakhouse.

items.notes: Herb-infused butter melted on top of your steak. Many steakhouses add this automatically. One pat = 100 cal of pure fat added to an already-cooked steak. Ask them to hold it.
items.lean_version: Ask for steak with NO butter on top. Most steakhouses will comply. Saves 100 cal instantly. The steak has plenty of flavor on its own.

steak_ranking_by_calories_per_8oz.verdict: BEST — leanest cut, highest protein-to-cal ratio
MODERATE — eat the tenderloin side first
HIGH — ask for end cut if you must
HIGHEST — same protein as filet, 340 MORE cal

best_orders: Shrimp cocktail + filet mignon + steamed broccoli = ~660 cal, 76g protein
Grilled chicken breast + asparagus + sweet potato = ~590 cal, 64g protein
Side salad + sirloin + steamed broccoli = ~685 cal, 59g protein
Lobster tail + filet (surf & turf, no butter) + asparagus = ~680 cal, 90g protein
worst_orders: Bread basket + French onion soup + ribeye (with butter) + loaded potato + creamed spinach = 2270 cal
Wedge salad + NY strip + mac & cheese + fries = 1980 cal
Bread + Caesar salad + prime rib + mashed potatoes + drawn butter = 2040 cal
key_swaps: Ribeye (780 cal) → Filet Mignon (440 cal) = save 340 cal, SAME protein
Loaded baked potato (470 cal) → Sweet potato plain (180 cal) = save 290 cal
Mac & cheese (520 cal) → Steamed broccoli (55 cal) = save 465 cal
Caesar salad (360 cal) → Side salad with vinaigrette (150 cal) = save 210 cal
French onion soup (370 cal) → Shrimp cocktail (160 cal) = save 210 cal, +22g protein
Bread basket (400 cal) → Skip bread = save 400 cal
Drawn butter (200 cal) → Lemon juice (5 cal) = save 195 cal
Mashed potatoes (350 cal) → Grilled asparagus (40 cal) = save 310 cal
smart_ordering_guide.eamons_rule: LOIN or ROUND in the name = lean cut. Filet mignon (tenderloin), sirloin, strip loin — all lean. Rib in the name (ribeye, prime rib) = fat cut. Same animal, same protein, wildly different calories.
